title: Death rates from different causes
subtitle: Reported annual death rate from different causes per 100,000 people, based on the [underlying cause](#dod:underlying-cause-of-death) listed on death certificates. Comparisons may be affected by differences in measurement.
note: To allow for comparisons between countries and over time, this metric is [age-standardized](#dod:age_standardized). All deaths in a country may not have been registered with a [cause of death](#dod:underlying-cause-of-death). Only a selection of categories are shown.
caption:
Death rates from different causes

Reported annual death rate from different causes per 100,000 people, based on the underlying cause listed on death certificates. Comparisons may be affected by differences in measurement.

This line chart tracks reported annual death rates from different causes in the United States from 1950 to 2022, measured per 100,000 people using an age-standardized metric. The vertical axis ranges from 0 to 600. The most prominent trend is the decline of cardiovascular diseases, represented by the orange line, which starts as the highest value above 500 in 1950 and drops steadily to finish near 130 in 2022. Cancers, shown in blue, maintain a relatively stable trajectory throughout the period, hovering between 140 and 160 before declining slightly to just under 100 by the end of the timeline. Neuropsychiatric conditions and drug use disorders, depicted in purple, show a distinct upward trend, rising from near the bottom of the chart in 1950 to approximately 75 in 2022. Unintentional injuries, in dark red, fluctuate within the lower range, generally staying between 30 and 60, with a noticeable spike occurring around 2020 that reaches nearly 80. Respiratory diseases, respiratory infections, digestive diseases, and intentional injuries form a cluster of lines in the lowest section of the chart, mostly remaining below the 50 mark. Infectious and parasitic diseases stay near the zero line for the majority of the period. The visualization includes only a selection of categories, and the data reflects underlying causes listed on death certificates.
